topic: Prediction-batch review of PRED-TOROID-003 (Schumann mode splitting Δf ≈ 0.336 Hz) post-EXP-136 integration

per_prediction_verdicts.verdict_on_verdict: standard_physics is defensible and correctly overrides the prior unfalsifiable classification
per_prediction_verdicts.reasoning: The analyst's core finding — that the dome's own derivation contains a 6.6× mathematical error — is correct and independently verifiable (see math_verification below). This finding is decisive for both the standard_physics classification and the is_genuinely_prospective=false call. However, see two substantive challenges below.

checks_performed.computation: For standard ellipse eccentricity e = sqrt(1 − b²/a²), e=0.66 → b/a = sqrt(1−0.4356) = 0.7513 → (1−0.7513)/(1+0.7513) = 0.1420

our_argument_summary: EXP-136 reclassifies PRED-TOROID-003 from unfalsifiable to standard_physics on the basis that (a) the dome's derivation of the 0.336 Hz splitting value contains two internal contradictions — a 6.6× eccentricity-ratio discrepancy and use of the globe-observed 7.83 Hz instead of the dome's own predicted ~22 Hz (WIN-038), and (b) real directional Schumann splitting is already explained by standard spherical-cavity physics (day-night ionospheric asymmetry; Sentman 1987, Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002, Pechony & Price 2004). The analyst also downgrades is_genuinely_prospective to false and flips data_predates_registration to true.

holes_found.description: The verdict 'standard_physics' may understate the problem. The dome does not merely restate a phenomenon already explained by standard physics — its stated derivation is internally inconsistent by a factor of 6.6×. A cleaner classification might be 'falsified' on the specific claim (a fixed geometric constant Δf ≈ 0.336 Hz), because observations show time-varying 0.1–0.5 Hz splitting — the dome's specific prediction (a fixed value of a cavity constant) is contradicted by the observed time-dependence. 'standard_physics' implies the dome prediction is non-distinguishing but valid within its stated framing; that framing (a constant) is itself empirically wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) keep 'standard_physics' but add a sentence noting the fixed-vs-variable falsification test explicitly in the tldr, OR (b) consider reclassifying as 'falsified' with a note that the phenomenon it names is separately explained by standard physics. Decider call. If keeping 'standard_physics', the 'notes' field should flag the mixed character (self-contradicted derivation + coincidentally-in-range value).

holes_found.description: Tension with c2 curmudgeon recommendation. In monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/ISS-703-sha256-forensics.c2.json, the curmudgeon explicitly said PRED-TOROID-003 is 'novel, testable, genuinely interesting — should be reclassified as genuinely prospective' and recommended 'Fair is fair. If a prediction is genuinely novel and testable, classify it honestly even if it comes from the dome model.' EXP-136 overrides this by keeping is_genuinely_prospective=false. The override is correct — the newly-surfaced 6.6× math error is decisive evidence the value was reverse-engineered, which c2 did not have — but EXP-136 does not acknowledge the prior recommendation or explain why it is being overruled.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence to the detail_reasoning or notes field stating: 'An earlier curmudgeon review (c2) characterized this prediction as genuinely prospective. The newly-identified mathematical inconsistency (6.6× eccentricity-ratio error combined with use of the globe-observed fundamental frequency) is the decisive evidence that the value was reverse-engineered rather than derived, supporting the is_genuinely_prospective=false classification.' This protects the review from later appearing inconsistent with itself.

holes_found.description: Objection-handling on non-standard eccentricity definition is adequate but could be sharper. A defender could argue: 'the dome's ovoid geometry isn't an ellipse — eccentricity may refer to (a−b)/a rather than the standard sqrt(1−b²/a²).' The analyst's response is 'it must be defined.' But we can go further: even under the polar-flattening convention f = (a−b)/a, f=0.66 gives b/a=0.34 and (a−b)/(a+b) = 0.66/1.34 = 0.493, which is 23× the 0.0215 claimed — so that reinterpretation makes the discrepancy WORSE, not better. The analyst's rebuttal is safe for any plausible geometric definition of 'eccentricity.'
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: strengthen the anticipated_objections response by noting that under ANY standard geometric convention (eccentricity, flattening, axial ratio), the stated e=0.66 cannot produce a ratio of 0.0215. This closes the 'maybe they meant a different definition' escape.

stronger_arguments.description: The magnetometer-orientation claim itself is physically odd. A Schumann cavity mode is a scalar resonance frequency — the energy of a given mode is the same regardless of which orientation you measure the magnetic field in. What a N–S vs E–W magnetometer sees differently is the MODAL STRUCTURE (polarization, phase), not the resonance frequency. If the dome is claiming different fundamental FREQUENCIES depending on magnetometer orientation at the same location, that is not what cavity resonance physics predicts — it would require the cavity to have anisotropic propagation, which is a much stronger claim than eccentricity. Observed directional peak-frequency differences in real ELF data arise from which mode dominates in which polarization, not from two different cavity fundamentals.
sources: Sentman, D. D. (1987). Magnetic elliptic polarization of Schumann resonances. Radio Science, 22(4), 595–606.
Nickolaenko, A. P., & Hayakawa, M. (2002). Resonances in the Earth-ionosphere cavity. Springer.

stronger_arguments.description: The 'factor of 2' in the formula Δf ≈ 0.0215 × 7.83 × 2 is labeled as 'implicit in resonance mode theory.' For an ellipsoidal cavity, mode splitting arises from breaking the azimuthal degeneracy (m-degeneracy) of a spherical-cavity mode. The standard result is Δf/f ≈ (3/2) × (a−b)/R × factor, with the numerical prefactor depending on mode numbers (l,m) — NOT a universal factor of 2. The dome's use of '×2' with no mode index appears to be a free parameter to make the number come out right, in addition to the other issues.
sources: Morse, P. M., & Feshbach, H. (1953). Methods of Theoretical Physics. Chapter 11 on cavity perturbation.

kernel_of_truth.description: Directional Schumann splitting IS a real, measurable phenomenon (0.1–0.5 Hz), and the dome correctly intuits that cavity asymmetry can produce mode splitting. The dome's prediction includes a specific numerical value and an attempted falsification criterion, which is methodologically better than most dome claims.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The derivation contains a 6.6× mathematical error and uses the globe-observed 7.83 Hz rather than the dome's own predicted ~22 Hz (from WIN-038), so the numerical match is almost certainly tuned rather than derived. The dome's stated falsification criterion ('zero directional splitting') is non-discriminating because standard physics also predicts non-zero splitting. Real splitting is time-varying (0.1–0.5 Hz depending on ionospheric conditions), which contradicts the dome's 'fixed geometric constant' claim.

advocate_mode.strongest_dome_defense: The specific value 0.336 Hz falls within observed range and is quantitatively stated, which is more specific than many dome claims. A defender could argue that (a) the 0.0215 figure may come from a different geometric convention or an empirically-fit parameter, (b) the use of 7.83 Hz is an 'observational anchor' rather than a derivation (the dome claims to reproduce this frequency in its own cavity, whether or not WIN-038 disputes that), and (c) within the dome framework, a prediction that happens to match observations is still a prediction — the 'tuning' accusation is not a disproof.
advocate_mode.why_this_defense_fails: The 6.6× mismatch cannot be rescued by redefining eccentricity under any standard geometric convention (see Hole #3 — flattening convention makes it worse). Claiming 7.83 Hz as an 'anchor' while also claiming the dome's cavity geometry produces it is exactly the self-contradiction WIN-038 documents; a defender cannot have both. Finally, a prediction produced by a demonstrably-inconsistent derivation that happens to match observations is the textbook definition of a post-hoc fit, not a derived prediction.

cross_win_consistency.check: Is EXP-136 consistent with our WIN-038 analysis?
cross_win_consistency.details: WIN-038 (verdict: Self-Contradicted) establishes that the dome's own cavity height function H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km predicts a Schumann fundamental of 14–42 Hz (depending on averaging), most plausibly ~22 Hz — not 7.83 Hz. EXP-136 correctly invokes this to show that the dome using 7.83 Hz in its splitting derivation is internally inconsistent with its own cavity. The two documents are mutually reinforcing.

recommended_edits: Add to detail_reasoning or notes: one sentence acknowledging the c2 curmudgeon recommendation and explaining why the newly-found math error overrides it.
Optionally sharpen the 'alternative eccentricity definition' objection by noting that under flattening convention f=(a−b)/a the discrepancy grows to 23×.

Decider call: consider whether 'falsified' (specific fixed-constant prediction contradicted by observed variability) is a better verdict than 'standard_physics'. If sticking with 'standard_physics', add a sentence making the fixed-vs-variable point explicit in the tldr.

citations_verified: Sentman 1987, Nickolaenko & Hayakawa 2002, Pechony & Price 2004 — real scientific references on directional Schumann splitting; analyst's characterization is consistent with the literature
citations_unchecked: Dome source formula 'Δf ≈ 0.0215 × 7.83 × 2' — not present in local raw-text/ snapshot; analyst presumably sourced from the live dome predictions page. The formula's arithmetic is confirmed (0.0215 × 7.83 × 2 = 0.3367). If the dome page does not in fact state the formula in this form, a separate issue should be filed; treating it as accurately quoted for now.

deeper_analysis: The math-error finding is the core contribution of EXP-136 and it is correct. A prediction whose stated derivation doesn't self-consistently reproduce its own quoted value is, by definition, not a derived prediction — it's a number attached to a formula that cannot produce it. Combined with the use of 7.83 Hz (a value WIN-038 already shows the dome cannot derive), the case is airtight that the number was reverse-engineered to match known observations. The verdict change from 'unfalsifiable' to 'standard_physics' is an unambiguous improvement. My reservations are about whether 'standard_physics' is the tightest available classification given that the specific quantitative claim (a fixed geometric-constant Δf) is contradicted by the observed time-variability of real Schumann splitting; a reader might reasonably expect this to be 'falsified.' The distinction is largely semantic but matters for tallies. This review's overall recommendation is to keep the verdict, add a brief acknowledgment of the superseded c2 recommendation, and make the fixed-vs-variable falsification test explicit in the user-visible TLDR.
notes_on_procedure: Reviewed under Priority Queue (queue_id 118), Step 4b prediction-batch procedure. One item, reviewed in full, per curmudgeon.md Step 0b 'one item per run' rule. No further queue work this run.
